coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient and broad-spectrum antibacterial carbon dots combat antibiotic resistance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Huimin
Wang, Panyong
Wu, Jie
Li, Xinlu
Du, Yuwei
Yan, Haiyang
You, Qiannan
Dong, Wenfei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections have become a major global public health issue, particularly with the emergence of multidrug-resistant strains. Therefore, developing non-antibiotic antimicrobial agents is crucial for treating drug-resistant bacterial infections. Building on previous research into natural products as novel antibacterial agents, this study synthesized curcumin-derived carbon dots using curcumin and ethylenediamine as raw materials through a hydrothermal method. The resulting carbon dots not only improved the water solubility and stability of curcumin but also exhibited highly efficient broad-spectrum antibacterial activity. Detailed investigations into the antibacterial performance and mechanisms of the carbon dots were conducted through experiments such as minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) determination, live/dead bacterial staining, morphological studies, nucleic acid concentration detection, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection. The results indicated that the carbon dots significantly damaged the structural integrity of bacteria and generated large amounts of ROS. They exhibited remarkable antibacterial effects against Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Staphylococcus aureus, and effectively inhibited drug-resistant MRSA. Their antibacterial efficacy was notably superior to that of broad-spectrum antibiotics such as chloramphenicol and Sulfadiazine. This study highlights the potential application of curcumin-derived carbon dots in combating bacterial infections and provides valuable insights for developing novel antibacterial agents derived from natural products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Curcumin
Carbon dots
Broad-spectrum antibacterial
Antibacterial agent

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2022YFC2403500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82172077, 22201298, and 62027825), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (No. ZR2022QB077 and ZR2022QB243), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), the Science and Technology Innovation Team Project of Foshan (2018IT100382), the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2019MC069), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20231207), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.2023SYHZ0037), Projects of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20210032) and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of Cur-CDs. (B) Size distributions of Cur-CDs. (C) FL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cur-CDs and Curcumin.
(A) Full survey XPS of the Cur-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s (B), N 1s (C), and O 1s (D) for the Cur-CDs.
Typical photographs of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and MRSA after treatment with different concentrations of Cur-CDs (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/mL) for 10 h.

Antibacterial ability of Cur-CDs. Typical photos of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and MRSA after 10 h of treatment with different PBS/Curcumin/Cur-CDs on LB medium. Abbreviations: (Cur) Curcumin.

Antibacterial ability of Cur-CDs. (A) Typical photos of E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and MRSA after 10 h of treatment with PBS/GM/C/CIP/SD/Cur-CDs on LB medium. (B) Bacterial viability on the agar plates in Fig. 5A. Abbreviations: (PBS) phosphate-buffered saline, (GM) gentamicin sulfate, (C) chloramphenicol, (CIP) ciprofloxacin, (SD) sulfadiazine, (CDs) Cur-CDs.
CLSM images of E. coli and P. aeruginosa stained by Calcein-AM and PI, respectively.
CLSM images of S. aureus and MRSA stained by Calcein-AM and PI, respectively.
SEM and TEM images of E. coli, P. aeruginosa (A), S. aureus, and MRSA (B) treated with Cur-CDs.
(A) Zeta potential of bacteria and Cur-CDs. (B) After incubation with Cur-CDs, the amount of bacterial nucleic acid leakage. (∗∗p < 0.01, n = 3, mean ± SD.).
Synthesis Route of Cur-CDs and the Application of Cur-CDs for Inhibiting Bacteria.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient and broad-spectrum antibacterial carbon dots combat antibiotic resistance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Life Sciences and Medicine
University of Science and Technology of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics
Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou 215163, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanophotonics and Biophotonics Key Laboratory of Jilin Province, School of Physics, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanophotonics and Biophotonics Key Laboratory of Jilin Province
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanophotonics and Biophotonics Key Laboratory of Jilin Province, School of Physics, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections have become a major global public health issue, particularly with the emergence of multidrug-resistant strains. Therefore, developing non-antibiotic antimicrobial agents is crucial for treating drug-resistant bacterial infections. Building on previous research into natural products as novel antibacterial agents, this study synthesized curcumin-derived carbon dots using curcumin and ethylenediamine as raw materials through a hydrothermal method. The resulting carbon dots not only improved the water solubility and stability of curcumin but also exhibited highly efficient broad-spectrum antibacterial activity. Detailed investigations into the antibacterial performance and mechanisms of the carbon dots were conducted through experiments such as minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) determination, live/dead bacterial staining, morphological studies, nucleic acid concentration detection, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection. The results indicated that the carbon dots significantly damaged the structural integrity of bacteria and generated large amounts of ROS. They exhibited remarkable antibacterial effects against Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Staphylococcus aureus, and effectively inhibited drug-resistant MRSA. Their antibacterial efficacy was notably superior to that of broad-spectrum antibiotics such as chloramphenicol and Sulfadiazine. This study highlights the potential application of curcumin-derived carbon dots in combating bacterial infections and provides valuable insights for developing novel antibacterial agents derived from natural products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacterial infections have emerged as a significant global public health threat. It is projected to cause 10 million deaths annually by 2050 [1]. However, traditional antibiotics are becoming increasingly ineffective due to the development of drug-resistant strains [2]. Although significant efforts have been made to develop new antibiotics, the increasing global use and improper application of antibiotics in medical practice have led to the constant emergence of major antibiotic-resistant bacteria in clinical settings. Therefore, finding new and efficient antibacterial agents has become particularly urgent.

The rise in antibiotic resistance has driven the development of new antibiotics. Equally important is the development of non-antibiotic drugs. Natural products have long captivated researchers owing to their broad biological activity and comparatively low toxicity. Curcumin, a natural polyphenol found in the rhizomes of plants, belonging to the ginger family, boasts a distinctive diketone structure. Beyond its role as a common food additive and coloring agent, curcumin has demonstrated significant biological activities, encompassing neuroprotective, anticancer, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties [3,4]. However, inherent challenges of curcumin, such as poor stability, easy degradation, and poor water solubility (only 13 μg/mL solubility in water), hinder its absorption, lead to swift metabolism, result in low bioavailability, and impose limitations on its biological and antibacterial activities [5]. In recent years, various materials, including micelles, nanoemulsions or microemulsions, liposomes, cyclodextrins, solid dispersions, among others, have been devised to augment its bioavailability [6].

In the post-antibiotic era, nanomaterials with high antibacterial efficiency and non-resistance have become attractive candidates for antibacterial applications. Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterials with a scale smaller than 10 nm, composed of a carbon nucleus and externally modified functional groups [7]. They have found widespread applications in various fields, including bioimaging [8–10], biosensors [11–13], antibacterial agent [14–18], catalysis [19,20], among others. The inherent non-toxicity, photostability, easy surface functionalization (to enhance bacterial interactions), and cost-effectiveness characteristics in synthesis make CDs superior to other antibacterial agents [21]. Moreover, compared to antibiotics, CDs involve diverse antibacterial mechanisms, significantly reducing the likelihood of bacterial resistance [22]. Various synthesis methods for antibacterial carbon dots have been developed. For instance, Yu [23] et al. prepared dual-functional carbon dots with antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties to promote wound healing. Miao [24] et al. utilized antimicrobial peptide carbon dots for the treatment of urinary tract infections. Nong [25] et al. employed natural biomolecular synthesis to create guanosine-based carbon dots for microbial treatment and diagnosis. Cui [26] et al. developed mild carbon dot TM-CD for food preservation. These examples highlight the significant potential of carbon dots as antibacterial agents in fields such as healthcare, environmental disinfection, and food preservation.

This study utilized curcumin and ethylenediamine as precursors to prepare curcumin-derived carbon dots (Cur-CDs) through a simple hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Cur-CDs are a new type of nano-antimicrobial material that combines the natural antimicrobial properties of curcumin with the unique physicochemical properties of carbon dots. Curcumin itself possesses antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant characteristics, while carbon dots are widely studied due to their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, ease of modification, and optical properties. While improving the stability, dispersibility, and water solubility of curcumin, Cur-CDs also enhance its antibacterial ability. Moreover, carbon dots do not easily lead to the development of antibiotic resistance when treating bacterial infections. The successful synthesis of the material was confirmed through characterization of its morphology and surface properties using TEM, XPS, FT-IR, and other methods. In antibacterial experiments, Cur-CDs demonstrated excellent activity against E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus. Notably, Cur-CDs showed remarkable inhibitory effects even against MRSA, a multidrug-resistant bacterium. The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of Cur-CDs against E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and MRSA were 40, 20, 20, and 60 μg/mL, respectively. It is worth mentioning that research has found the antibacterial effect of Cur-CDs to be superior to some antibiotics, including chloramphenicol, ciprofloxacin, and sulfamethoxazole. Overall, our findings indicate that Cur-CDs possess high biocompatibility and efficient antibacterial effects, capable of inhibiting and killing common bacteria, and hold potential as novel antibacterial agents.
The reagents, apparatus, and methods are provided in the Supplementary Materials.

Carbon dots derived from curcumin (Cur-CDs) were synthesized through a hydrothermal method involving the reaction of curcumin and ethylenediamine in ultrapure water at 200 °C for 8 h. The morphology of the synthesized Cur-CDs was meticulously investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 A, TEM images illustrated well-dispersed spherical carbon dots with a size distribution ranging from 2 to 4 nm, exhibiting an average diameter of approximately 2.92 nm (Fig. 1B). Further confirmed the microstructure and phase composition of carbon dots through X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy. A characteristic peak of graphite carbon at 2θ = 24° was observed in the XRD pattern, corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphite structure [27,28] (Fig. S1A). As shown in Fig. S1B, the Raman spectrum shows that the D band and G band of carbon reach their peaks at 1407 and 1579 cm-1, respectively, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.99, indicating that the carbon dots have fewer defects in the graphite carbon phase and a higher degree of graphitization. This indicates that the carbon dots have good crystallinity and structural integrity [29–31].

In-depth characterization revealed the superior optical properties of Cur-CDs. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were determined to be 450 nm and 520 nm, respectively (Fig. S2). Fluorescence spectra recorded at different excitation wavelengths demonstrated the excitation-independent fluorescence characteristics within the range of 390 nm–470 nm (Fig. 1C). To determine the stability of carbon dots, we measured the fluorescence intensity of Cur-CDs at different pH levels and temperatures. The results are shown in Fig. S3, where the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots showed no significant fluctuations at different pH levels and temperatures. This indicates that the synthesized carbon dots are relatively stable under various conditions. The aqueous solution of Cur-CDs, depicted in Fig. 1D, exhibited a distinctive brownish-yellow hue and emitted a vibrant green fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of curcumin showcased a weak absorption peak at 264 nm, attributed to the π--π∗ transition of the CC bond [32]. A prominent peak at 428 nm was observed, corresponding to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic ring [33], signifying the maximum absorption wavelength of curcumin. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the aqueous solution of Cur-CDs displayed an absorption peak at 236 nm, associated with the π-π∗ transition. The peak at 348 nm was assigned to the n-π∗ transition of CO and –NO [34], while the subtle absorption peak at 414 nm originated from the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic ring. The inclusion of ethylenediamine during carbon dot synthesis induced changes in the UV–visible absorption spectrum, indicating size effects, electron resonance effects, and the incorporation of nitrogen elements. This attests that the synthesized carbon dots retained the cyclic structure of the curcumin precursor while introducing new functional groups.

To delve further into the properties of Cur-CDs, we conducted a thorough analysis of the surface functional groups using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). The XPS spectrum of Cur-CDs (Fig. 2 A) revealed the elemental composition, highlighting the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) – a composition consistent with curcumin and ethylenediamine. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) unfolded three discernible peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.7 eV, corresponding to sp2C, C–C/C–N, and CO bonds, respectively [35]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) manifested two distinct peaks at 398.8 and 400.3 eV, indicative of C–N and N–H bonds [9,36,37]. As for the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), it exhibited three peaks at 530.7, 532.1, and 533.0 eV, attributed to CO, C–O, and C–OH/C–O–C functionalities [38]. These XPS spectra collectively affirmed the existence of carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and nitrogen-containing groups in Cur-CDs.

The functional groups of curcumin and Cur-CDs were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Fig. S4). Common absorption peaks at 3018, 2860, and 1480 cm−1 were attributed to C–H stretching vibrations [33,39], alongside bending vibrations of aromatic C–H bonds. The peak of 3500 cm−1 in curcumin corresponded to the stretching vibration of –OH [2,33,40]. Peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 1601 cm−1 emanated from stretching vibrations of CC and CO bonds [33,39,40]. Absorption peaks at 1427 cm−1, 1153 cm−1, and 807 cm−1 denoted bending vibrations of -O-H, stretching vibrations of -C-O [33], and bending vibrations of –CC [39], respectively. In Cur-CDs, the 3280 cm−1 peak signifies –OH and potentially N–H stretching vibrations, implying the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups. Peaks at 1567 cm−1, 1304 cm−1, and 816 cm−1 were associated with stretching vibrations of CO, C–N, and C–O, and planar bending vibrations of C–H [41], respectively. The indication of –COOH suggested the presence of carboxyl groups, predominantly due to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the OC–O group [42]. These oxygen-containing functionalities contributed to the exceptional dispersion characteristics of Cur-CDs in solution. The FTIR spectrum analysis and XPS analysis both indicate the presence of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups. Together, they confirm the successful synthesis of Cur-CDs, which retained the structural motifs of curcumin while introducing new functional groups (-COOH, -N-H).

To evaluate the antibacterial performance of Cur-CDs against common bacteria, we selected two types of Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli and P. aeruginosa) and two types of Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and MRSA). Among them, MRSA is a highly toxic drug-resistant S. aureus. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) method was used to test the antibacterial activity. Four types of bacteria were co-cultured with different concentrations of Cur-CDs solutions in LB medium, and the cultured bacterial solution was then dotted onto LB agar plates. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . As the concentration of Cur-CDs increased, the number of colonies formed on the plate decreased. For E. coli, no colonies were generated on the LB medium when the concentration of Cur-CDs reached 40 μg/mL. For P. aeruginosa and S. aureus, no colonies were produced on the LB medium at a concentration of 20 μg/mL. For MRSA, no growth was observed on the culture medium at a concentration of 60 μg/mL. From this, it can be concluded that the minimum inhibitory concentrations of Cur-CDs against E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and MRSA are 40 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, and 60 μg/mL, respectively. This indicates that Cur-CDs can efficiently inhibit common bacteria and also have excellent antibacterial effects against drug-resistant bacteria.

First, we used the plate coating method to compare the antibacterial effects of Cur-CDs with raw curcumin. At the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), the carbon dots were incubated with the bacterial solution, diluted to the appropriate concentration, and then applied to LB agar plates. The antibacterial performance of Cur-CDs was evaluated based on the number of colonies formed on LB plates. As shown in Fig. 4 , for Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli and P. aeruginosa), the LB plates of the PBS group were filled with colonies. The number of colonies on LB plates treated with curcumin was comparable to that of the PBS control group, while no colonies were formed on LB plates treated with Cur-CDs. Similarly, after co-culturing Cur-CDs with Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and MRSA), no colonies grew on agar plates. This demonstrates that Cur-CDs have the same antibacterial effect against Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and MRSA).

This further confirms that Cur-CDs can inhibit bacterial growth at the minimum inhibitory concentration, consistent with the results of the previous MIC experiment. It is evident that, compared to raw curcumin, Cur-CDs significantly improve the antibacterial effects against four common bacteria. One of the reasons for this is that the solubility is greatly increased after the synthesis of CDs from curcumin, which enhances the antibacterial effect.

To comprehensively evaluate the antibacterial performance of the synthesized carbon dots, we selected four broad-spectrum antibiotics (gentamicin sulfate, chloramphenicol, ciprofloxacin, and sulfadiazine) and compared their antibacterial effects with Cur-CDs. The results are shown in Fig. 5 .

At a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 40 μg/mL of Cur-CDs against E. coli, sterile colonies were formed in both the carbon dot group and the gentamicin sulfate group. Fig. 5B also indicates that the bacterial survival rate is only 1 % (with an antibacterial efficiency of 99 %). In contrast, colonies in the other antibiotic groups grew normally. At a MIC of 20 μg/mL of Cur-CDs against P. aeruginosa, both the carbon dot group and the gentamicin sulfate group showed significant antibacterial effects, with an inhibition efficiency of up to 99 %. Ciprofloxacin also exhibited a strong antibacterial effect at this concentration, with an efficiency of 98 %. At a concentration of 20 μg/mL, carbon dots, gentamicin sulfate, and ciprofloxacin effectively inhibited the formation of S. aureus colonies, achieving an antibacterial efficiency of 99 %. At 60 μg/mL, carbon dots, gentamicin sulfate, chloramphenicol, and ciprofloxacin effectively inhibited MRSA, with an antibacterial efficiency of 99 %. Based on these experimental results, it can be inferred that our synthesized Cur-CDs have excellent antibacterial effects, surpassing those of chloramphenicol, ciprofloxacin, and sulfadiazine at the same concentration. This indicates that Cur-CDs have great potential for application as antibacterial agents.

In addition, the antibacterial effects of other nanomaterials were summarized and organized, as shown in Table S1. From the table, it can be seen that although metal nanomaterials exhibit strong antibacterial effects, they also have potential biological toxicity, high costs, and certain environmental impacts. Other nanomaterials demonstrate good antibacterial effects but also have disadvantages such as instability and difficulty in degradation. Overall, carbon dots have shown good antibacterial effects, strong biocompatibility, simple synthesis, and suitability for various bacteria. Therefore, the preparation of carbon dots from curcumin can fully leverage its antibacterial properties.

Furthermore, we also compared the antibacterial performance of Cur-CDs with other carbon dots. The results are shown in Table S2. From the table, it can be seen that Cur-CDs have a lower inhibitory MIC concentration against E. coli and S. aureus, and they do not require additional light exposure to assist in sterilization, demonstrating the excellent antibacterial properties of Cur-CDs. It is evident that Cur-CDs have the potential to serve as an antibacterial agent.

To further study the destructive effects of Cur-CDs on four types of bacteria, live/dead staining methods were employed. This analysis aimed to elucidate the toxicity and degree of structural damage induced by Cur-CDs. The Calcein-AM green fluorescent dye can label all bacteria, whereas propidium iodide (PI) can penetrate ruptured cell membranes and show enhanced red fluorescence when binding to nucleic acids. When the two dyes are mixed in a certain proportion, green fluorescence dominates in normal cells, while cells with damaged membranes exhibit significant red fluorescence [43]. The CLSM images of bacteria stained with Calcein-AM and PI are shown in Figs. 6 and 7
. For these four bacteria, the control group exhibited overall green fluorescence, indicating minimal bacterial death due to cell membrane damage. In contrast, bacteria treated with Cur-CDs displayed bright red fluorescence, indicating severe cell membrane damage and low survival rates.

These findings suggest that Cur-CDs have strong antibacterial effects on both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, consistent with the results of plate coating. Overall, the results strongly demonstrate that the prepared Cur-CDs possess outstanding bacterial inactivation abilities, highlighting their potential as effective antibacterial agents.

Before advancing the application of the synthesized Cur-CDs in biological contexts, their compatibility with NIH-3T3 and A549 cells was thoroughly examined using the WST-1 assay. As depicted in Fig. S5, following a 24-h co-incubation with Cur-CDs solutions spanning concentrations from 0 to 80 μg/mL, the cell viability consistently surpassed 70 %. Even at these heightened concentrations, cell growth exhibited no discernible hindrance, underscoring that the introduction of Cur-CDs did not significantly impede cellular proliferation. Consequently, the in vitro toxicity against NIH-3T3 and A549 cells induced by Cur-CDs can be considered negligible. This substantiates the synthesized Cur-CDs' commendable attributes of low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility.

E. coli and S. aureus were used as representatives of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria to study the antibacterial mechanisms of Cur-CDs, respectively. To delve into the precise antibacterial mechanism of Cur-CDs, we employed scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) to scrutinize morphological alterations in bacteria pre- and post-Cur-CDs treatment. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , before treatment with Cur-CDs, the SEM images of the four bacterial strains all exhibited clear edges and intact cell wall structures. The TEM images also revealed that the edges of the Gram-negative bacteria E. coli and P. aeruginosa are distinct, with intact flagella. The surface morphology of the Gram-positive bacteria was smooth and complete. However, after treatment with Cur-CDs, the SEM images showed defects at the edges of the bacterial cells, damage to the cell wall/membrane structures, and leakage of intracellular contents for all four types of bacteria. The TEM images also clearly displayed structural damage to the bacteria, and the flagella of E. coli and P. aeruginosa had detached. The experimental results indicate that Cur-CDs can disrupt the cell wall/membrane structures of bacteria, thereby inhibiting their growth.

Furthermore, we examined the surface potential of E. coli and S. aureus. As depicted in Fig. 9 A, Cur-CDs exhibited a negative ζ potential, and both E. coli and S. aureus displayed negative ζ potentials as well. This indicates that Cur-CDs do not disrupt bacterial structures through electrostatic interactions with the bacterial surface, given the negative charge of Cur-CDs and the bacterial surface [44,45]. Research has found that charged carbon dots (whether positive or negative) exhibit a stronger ability to induce bacterial destruction and oxidative stress compared to neutral carbon dots [46]. However, many reported positively charged CDs tend to adhere to the surface of target bacteria through electrostatic interactions and then exert antibacterial effects. Due to the negative charge of most biofilms, these positively charged CDs may cause adverse cytotoxicity and hemolysis to red blood cells [47]. Therefore, negatively charged carbon dots may have higher biosafety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) in bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: ROS disrupts many aspects of cell physiology, including lipids, proteins, nucleotides, and metabolites. Intrinsically generated ROS are considered major triggers of bactericidal stress and apoptotic effector molecules [46]. The production of endogenous ROS in bacteria was investigated in our study. We utilized 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCFH) as a probe to ascertain the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). DCFH-DA, a non-fluorescent compound, permeates the cell membrane and undergoes hydrolysis by intracellular esterase, converting it into DCFH. DCFH, incapable of membrane penetration, is thus sequestered within the cell. Intracellular ROS transforms non-fluorescent DCFH into fluorescent DCF. The fluorescence of DCF serves as an indicator of intracellular ROS levels. As depicted in Fig. S6, within 30 min of incubation with Cur-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the solution containing E. coli and S. aureus significantly increased, indicating the production of a large amount of reactive oxygen species. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the bacterial solution incubated with PBS did not change significantly within 150 min, and no reactive oxygen species were produced. It can be inferred that the presence of Cur-CDs promotes the generation of ROS within bacteria, suggesting that Cur-CDs can disrupt bacterial structure and kill bacteria by inducing ROS production.

Subsequently, we probed the impact of Cur-CDs on internal bacterial nucleic acids. Following the co-incubation of bacteria with Cur-CDs, we quantified the concentration of leaked nucleic acids in the solution. The nucleic acid concentration of the control group was set at 100 %. As delineated in Fig. 9B, for E. coli, co-incubation with Cur-CDs resulted in a 40 % higher concentration of leaked nucleic acids compared to the group not co-incubated with Cur-CDs. After incubation with Cur-CDs, the concentration of nucleic acid leakage increased by 20 % for S. aureus. These results are consistent with those observed using SEM. It can be concluded that Cur-CDs cause damage to bacterial structures, leading to leakage of internal nucleic acids and exerting significant destructive effects on bacteria.

The above research results indicate that the synthesized Cur-CDs have a significant inhibitory effect on E. coli and S. aureus. It can be inferred that the antibacterial effect of Cur-CDs is primarily achieved by disrupting the bacterial cell membrane/wall structure and entering the cells. Additionally, reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by carbon dots cause oxidative damage to bacterial cell membranes, increasing their permeability, which also leads to cell death [48]. This disruption leads to the generation of a large amount of reactive oxygen species within the bacteria, which destroys their internal structures, causes leakage of nucleic acid molecules and other contents, and ultimately kills the bacteria. Carbon dots may interact with proteins and enzymes within the bacteria [49,50], causing them to lose function, and affecting the normal metabolism and growth of the bacteria [51]. Carbon dots may affect the bacterial respiratory chain, blocking energy metabolism, causing bacteria to be unable to obtain sufficient energy and resulting in death [52]. Additionally, relevant studies have shown that CDs can enhance bacterial surface adsorption through van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonds, leading to bacterial death by disrupting cell membranes, producing ROS, and binding to bacterial DNA [53].

In this study, Cur-CDs with improved water solubility compared to curcumin were synthesized. Cur-CDs exhibit excellent fluorescence performance and biocompatibility. Moreover, comparisons revealed that the antibacterial effect of Cur-CDs is not inferior to common antibiotics. Therefore, Cur-CDs have the potential to serve as novel antibacterial agents. Live/dead staining and morphological studies demonstrated that Cur-CDs can damage the cell walls and membranes of bacteria, leading to the efflux of internal substances from bacterial cells and thereby killing the bacteria. Based on bacterial morphology analysis, reactive oxygen species detection, and nucleic acid concentration detection, it can be inferred that the synthesized Cur-CDs induce ROS production by disrupting the structure of bacterial membranes/walls, leading to leakage of bacterial contents (nucleic acids, etc.) and thus inhibiting bacterial growth.

In summary, we have synthesized a novel antibacterial agent Cur-CDs. Compared with other antibiotics, Cur-CDs offer advantages such as a simple synthesis method, low cost, good antibacterial effect, reliable safety, and reduced susceptibility to drug resistance, making them excellent antibacterial agents. This material provides new approaches for combating bacterial infections, bacterial resistance, and other antibacterial fields. In the future, we can also modify the surface of carbon dots to specifically target bacterial cells and enhance antibacterial efficiency. Future studies can explore the application of Cur-CDs in actual medical environments, including the treatment of infections and disinfectants. However, before that, the specific antibacterial mechanism of Cur-CDs still needs further research, and their effectiveness and safety in clinical environments also require long-term experimental research.

This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2022YFC2403500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82172077, 22201298, and 62027825), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (No. ZR2022QB077 and ZR2022QB243), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), the Science and Technology Innovation Team Project of Foshan (2018IT100382), the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2019MC069), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20231207), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.2023SYHZ0037), Projects of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20210032) and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

Huimin Miao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Panyong Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jie Wu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Xinlu Li: Methodology. Yuwei Du: Methodology. Haiyang Yan: Methodology, Data curation. Qiannan You: Supervision, Investigation. Wenfei Dong: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Li Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126926.
